spec_match.rationale: Explicitly grounded in the chromestatus motivation quote, walks three real-world scenarios contrasting pre-130 destination-only selection vs post-130 scope-pre-filtering. The before/after attribution decisions reflect spec behaviour.

interactive.rationale: Three scenario presets, replay/step controls, animated timeline, side-by-side before/after verdicts that update per scenario.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises a user journey contrasting pre-Chrome 130 attribution with scope-aware attribution — page delivers exactly that.

edge_cases.rationale: The conservative all-sources-mismatch outcome is now covered by the sibling scope-overconservative concept and linked from the feature index. This page still lacks a neutral no-collision baseline, and its pre-130 selection helper should be tightened.

design_system.rationale: CSS vars throughout, blue/rose/emerald accents used semantically, no static-card-only pages, --accent-amber used appropriately.

openQuestions.title: Add a 'no-collision' baseline scenario
openQuestions.detail: All three current scenarios produce wrong→right outcomes. Add a fourth where there's only one source — both pre-130 and Chrome 130 credit it correctly. That establishes that scopes aren't always needed and gives the visitor a sense of when to use them vs not.

openQuestions.title: Tighten the pre-130 selection model in code
openQuestions.detail: The script sorts by priority then picks last — but the comment says 'most recent w/ priority tiebreak' and the actual code just falls back to the last entry. Spec behaviour is highest-priority then most-recent within priority. Make the logic match the documented model so the demo is technically honest.

summary: DevTools MCP verification confirmed the sibling scope-overconservative page exists for the conservative no-attribution case. It now keeps the trigger input editable, treats omitted trigger attribution_scopes as broadly eligible, and contrasts explicit scope misses with exact matches.
